title: Love, Innocence and the Word Cock
blurb: A Perrier-nominated exploration of innocence and first loves.

blurbs.text:
‘Rude, thoughtful, utterly hilarious’ *Evening Standard*. 
‘Very good stand-up indeed’ *Guardian*. 
‘Brilliantly funny’ *Metro*.
‘Kitson will save you from yourselves’ *Time Out*.
Daniel is very good at comedy. Watch him do some.

title: Something
blurb: The show that won the Perrier Award - a melancholy masterpiece.
development:
2002	Edinburgh Festival Fringe
2003	UK Tour “Over-rated and on Tour”

blurbs.text: Everyone said he was good last year. You should come if you like self-indulgent whismey on the verge of critical backlash. ‘His developing reputation is akin to the buzz around Oasis playing Glastonbury in 1994.’ *Times.*

title: A Made Up Story
blurb: A dreamy, indie fairytale with video accompaniment.
development:
Daniel Kitson’s first theatre show. He did several radio and text interviews to promote it, in which he explained that he’d created it in an effort to ward off the people who’d joined his fanbase as a result of his recurring role on the TV show *Phoenix Nights* - and, to a lesser extent, those who’d started following him because he’d won the Perrier Award the year before. He believed the type of people who liked *Phoenix Nights* would not like a sensitive theatre piece, and the people who liked him because he’d won a comedy award would expect more of the same type of comedy the next year, and he wanted to put both those groups off.

August 2003 - full run at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival
November 2003 - run at the Soho Theatre, London

blurbs.text: A one-man thing, not really stand-up, some film, in the round. It could make your heart explode and leave you breathless with emotion. Or it could be really very bad.

title: Lover, Thinker, Artist and Prophet
blurb: A challenging follow-up to his Perrier win.

blurbs.text: A challenging follow-up to his Perrier win.

title: Dancing
development:
Explanation, within the show, for why he did this show in Edinburgh instead of the tour show that he’d done earlier in 2004: “I’ll be honest with you about the genesis of this show. What happened was, I was in Australia and I was a bit down for a variety of reasons. I was a little bit heartbroken, I was a little bit bored, I was a little bit homesick. I got a phone call from my agent. She phones me up and she goes, ‘What do you want to do in Edinburgh?’ Now you see, all of us, I think, for large portions of our life, have a peculiar feeling of hollowness, somewhere inside us. Not quite as far back as our backs, just behind our tummies. […] When Hannah phoned me up and said, ‘What do you want to do in Edinburgh?’, I was blaming comedy for this peculiar feeling of hollowness. Going, ‘Oh, I’ve just done too much comedy, I’m doing comedy all the time, I’m bored of comedy. I’ll just do less comedy. I’m doing gigs every night, my life feels empty because I’m doing so much comedy, I’ll do less comedy.’ Now remarkably, taking something out of a life that feels empty doesn’t make it feel more full. Retrospectively, I can see where I blundered. But at the time, I was flummoxed by how my clever plan was failing to take effect. So, she said ‘Hi, Daniel, what do you want to do in Edinburgh?’ And I’m like, ‘I don’t fucking care. Just put my name up somewhere, the cunts will come, won’t they? Fuck them, Hannah, fuck them all, every last fucking one of them.’ But since then, I’ve perked up.”

August 29, 2004: released on Bandcamp


Bandcamp notes:
“This is a recording from the last night of my edinburgh festival run in 2004. It was recorded at the stand.

And much like the other stand recording available here - I wanted to charge 27p (a funny amount) but 40p is the minimum chargeable. Also the number of free downloads is limited so that Bandcamp can cover their own costs. So i've made it a no minimum "pay what you can" type thing.

If you can afford 40p pay that. If you can't - dont. If, however, the free downloads run out it will have to be 40p all round. But please dont pay any more than 40 p for this.

To be completely honest, i'd rather you just took this for free and bought The Ballad of Roger and Grace. But. Of course, its up to you.

Oh, and, this show is already available for free.on my website and in itunes as a podcast, unless that has stopped working.

That's all the information you need really.”

blurbs.text: A foolish man, a broken man, and a hero to few. daniel kitson returns to edinburgh determined to hide from audiences, critics and any sort of tangible success. work in progress. of sorts. let’s be all we can be.

title: Daniel Kitson 11:30 pm at The Stand
blurb: Daniel Kitson 11:30 pm at The Stand

blurbs.text: Wooo-hoooo that’s right Edinburgh, it’s time once more to laugh through your hands and glance at your friends for approval whilst a man stands before you on a well lit plinth and says things. Boom!

development: The author and musician Sam Taplin would later reference aspects of his childhood he shared with Kitson in his song Dan on the album Farewell to the doomed romantics, released in August 2013.[1]

reviews.snippet: He has a fragile ego, he admits, as several hundred people gaze at him in awe.

title: Stories for the Wobbly-Hearted

synopsis.text: A collection of interlinked love stories told in the dark, exploring themes of love, loss, and mixtapes.
Won a Scotsman Fringe First Award and later took to Brits Off Broadway.

blurb: Interlinked love stories that won a Fringe First.

reviews.snippet: They're simple, limpid tales told with boundless empathy.
The tales are as winsome as they are whimsical. Some are a touch predictable in their outcome, and a couple outstay their welcome.

development:
Won Scotsman Fringe First Award

Performed in 2006: re-staged

Performed at the Melbourne International Comedy Festival in 2005. Opened at the Traverse Theatre for the 2005 Edinburgh Festival Fringe and won a Scotsman Fringe First Award.

blurbs.text: Stories of heroic loneliness, optimistic sadness and broken love. told by lamp light.

title: Weltanschauung
development:
Late 2005 - worked out some material while compering at the Brixton Hobgoblin, which became the basis for Weltanschuung

January to March 2006 - performed it in UK theatres, including:
- Brighton Komedia (January 5)
- Gulbenkian Arts Centre, Kent (January 19)
- Unity Theatre, Liverpool (January 24)
- Birmingham Glee Club (February 1)
- Town Hall in Loughborough, Leicestershire (February 16)
- Lemon Tree in Aberdeen (February 17)
- A run from February 28 to March 4 at the Battersea Arts Centre

April 2006 - Melbourne International Comedy Festival, followed by performing in Sydney and Brisbane in April/May

May 28, 2006 - headlined the Brixton Hobgoblin (compered by Ivor Dembina), tried to perform Weltanschuung to refresh the material before Regent’s Park, but got a drunk and rowdy crowd that heckled him into only getting to do tiny bits of the material and mostly just fought with the audience for 90 minutes before giving up

June 11, 2006 - made up the first half of the performance in Regent’s Park Open Air Theatre, with Gavin Osborn

October 7-29, 2005 - run at The Hen and Chickens Theatre

October 14, 2012 - released on Bandcamp https://danielkitson.bandcamp.com/album/weltanschauung-2006

Notes on the Bandcamp release:
[This version of Weltanschauung] was recorded in Sydney at a Retired Servicemens League Club in Newtown in 2006. The venue is no longer a venue and the show is full of things im slightly ashamed of now - outdated references to myspace, a bit that has the exact rhythm and delivery of a Bill Hicks bit. Factual Inaccuracies all over the shop, a misquoted film, the simplification of a friends work, an embarrasingly pubescent call to arms and the misnaming of Geoffrey Chaucer as "Henry".

Having said all that, even though it's scattered with things i no longer think or believe, it's got stuff in it that i still really like and am pretty proud of. It was the first show where i realised i could work things out onstage, that i could disagree with myself and that i didn't need to know what i thought in order to talk about things. I could talk about not knowing what i thought. I could use the contradictions. It was my most polemic and opinionated piece of work up until that point and felt, to me at least, like an exciting development.

Realistically though, I think it's probably just a mildly interesting period piece for the eager completist.

blurbs.text:
It's Henry's last day at work. Surrounded by thousands of discarded or undelivered compilation tapes he will start, at last, to listen. And he'll find in the tapes unheard stories, stories about himself, about people he knows. About everything.

A new show by Daniel Kitson about memory, hope and a man's last day in a job that has never existed.

It's Henry's last day at work. Surrounded by thousands of discarded or undelivered compilation tapes he will start, at last, to listen. and in the tapes he'll find unheard stories, stories about himself, about people he knows. about everything. a new show

title: It's the Fireworks Talking

synopsis.text: A stand-up show that won the Barry Award at the Melbourne International Comedy Festival.

development: Won the Barry Award at the 2007 Melbourne International Comedy Festival. Toured UK, Australia, New York and Paris in 2007.

blurbs.text:
With stories about swimming in the sea, the courage of kindness, and telling your parents you love them. Words will swell with hope and float into the sky until they explode like fireworks and suddenly your tummy is flipping, your heart is thumping, your eyes are wet and your skin is tingling with some forgotten magic.

At least, that's the plan.

title: The Impotent Fury of the Privileged

synopsis.text: Often critically acclaimed, reviewers noted the 'predictability of its excellence'. A masterful display of stand-up craft.

development:
Performed in 2008: re-staged

Played work-in-progress gigs in Jan/Feb 2008 before a 28-date run in May & June. Released as an audio download in January 2017.

blurbs.text:
An angry, frustrated and beautiful new show about wanting the world to change but not doing enough to change it.

The world is a saddening mess, of unfathomable complexity and the simplest cruelty. and yet, rather than raging or weeping, most of us still manage to eat snacks, do crosswords and occasionally wash. But what if, what if we heave our compassion from wherever its buried and with courage enough to seem naïve or underinformed or oblivious to futility, we reach for something better. Something more engaged, more humane and less snack based.

The world is a saddening mess, of unfathomable complexity and cruelty and yet rather than raging or weeping, most of us still manage to eat snacks, do crosswords and occasionally wash. An angry, frustrated and beautiful new show about wanting the world to change but not doing enough to change it.

title: Untitled, AKA The Revenge of Heckmondwyke
development: With Gavin Osborn

blurbs.text: The Revenge of Heckmondwyke

title: The Ballad of Roger and Grace
blurb: The Ballad of Roger and Grace
development: with Gavin Osborn

blurbs.text:
Nearly twenty years ago, Daniel Kitson and Gavin Osborn wrote their first show together for one night at Regents Park Open Air Theatre.

After that, because they liked it so much, they toured the show around Australia and released it digitally about ten years ago before putting it out on vinyl, maybe two years ago, and now, without the excuse of a specific anniversary or a vinyl re-issue or indeed any reason at all beyond wanting to be able to pay for things and not really having any new ideas – Daniel and Gavin are coming back together to perform The Ballad of Roger and Grace.

It is, to be fair to it, a lovely and funny story with beautiful and heart breaking songs,

But also, these days, you simply have to monetise your back catalogue.

title: Stories for the Starlit Sky
development:
Three interlinked stories with music by Gavin Osborn

Performed in 2010: With Gavin Osborn

Performed in 2015: With Gavin Osborn

Performed in 2017: re-staged

blurbs.text: Three interlinked stories about love (obviously), staying up all night (naturally) and a village populated by retired assasins, performed by Daniel Kitson and Gavin Osborn.

Sneak through late night London; all around, people letting themselves down or heading home, done with the day, thinking of bed and of breakfast, oblivious to you, to us, hiding in a park at midnight, hurling tales of love and hope and courage at the sky. Voices soaring from between trees to paint tears on the face of the moon; words, like fire in the night, laughter exploding through the dark. The day not done, not yet, the air thick with magic on the cusp of tomorrow.

Daniel Kitson and Gavin Osborn return to Regent’s Park Open Air Theatre for this ridiculously exciting notion. Born three years ago in giddy post show euphoria, resurrected last year in a similar state of wonder and pushed through a wintery gloop of logistical troubles to this rather wonderful folly.
The Idea: To conceive, write and perform three separate stories for three separate summer shows, all starting at midnight and lasting a little over an hour. A twinkling thought is about to become a magical reality. All conceivable obstacles to success have been moved, avoided or obliterated save for one trifling concern. Will anybody actually come to a park at midnight to listen to a story?

Stories for the Starlit Sky – A Tour of a Trilogy.
 
Daniel Kitson and Gavin Osborn.
 
Daniel Kitson (Me) and Gavin Osborn (Gavin) return to the wide open road for the first time in ages. Ages. So long. Years. Oh god. Nothing stays the same does it? Dust on the wind. That’s all we are.
 
Anyway – here we are. The D Machine (Me) and G Unit (Gavin), back on tour. Doing it right.
 
In 2009 (Seriously, isn’t the passage of time horrifying?) we wrote a trilogy of story (Me) and song (Gavin) ultra combo mega shows called “Stories for the Starlit Sky” and comprising three interlinked stories about love (obviously), staying up all night (naturally) and a village populated by retired assasins (yeah, that makes sense).
 
We performed them in Regents Park Open Air Theatre on three separate summer nights, one in June, one in July, one in August and all of them at midnight. There were indeed stars in the sky. Some people brought flasks.
 
A couple of years later, we whacked them out over consecutive nights at the Latitude festival from the middle of a lake in a slightly futile battle against the ambient bass of various DJ sets and whooping, intoxicated infants.
 
We also did them once to raise money for Forest Fringe and so I could impress a woman who went home after the first story.
 
Most recently in July of this year (2015, obviously guys) Regents Park Open Air Theatre had a spare night after a late drop out - I rewrote the stories a bit (one of them really quite a lot) Gavin practiced his songs and we performed all three of the shows in one night. One after the other.
 
It was great.
 
So, that’s what this (indoor) tour will be, the whole blinking trilogy on one night complete with two intervals.
 
Boom. Break. Boom. Break. Wallop. Hometime.

3 Stories and 14 Songs

title: The Interminable Suicide of Gregory Church

synopsis.text: The story of Kitson finding a pile of letters in a charity shop and deciding to reply to them, leading to an investigation into the life of Gregory Church.
Another Scotsman Fringe First Award winner.

blurb: An investigation into a life through lost letters.
development: Toured in late 2010 and the first half of 2011.

blurbs.text: Gregory had fifty seven letters to write. He'd never written that many letters, not in one go. In fact, he'd never written a single letter and it was taking significantly longer than he'd anticipated. He'd started, full of optimism, curiously enough, at 9am and now here he was 8 hours later half way through letter twenty four. He glanced at his watch and then at the noose hanging over his head.Gregory sighed. Had he known how long suicide letters take, he thought, he wouldn't have cancelled the milk for the morning. a story about a death postponed by life.
When a man decides to die, he should put his business in order. He should cancel direct debits, empty the fridge, clean the house and, thinks Gregory, he should write letters.

title: We Are Gathered Here
development: Plus UK tour

blurbs.text:
Everyone you have ever known will die.

And so will you.

And yet we dance in the looming shadows of mortality, we dance and we talk and we eat and we argue. We read books. We care for people. We buy houses. We plant trees and we start to drive and we learn how to make milk frothy. Because something, somewhere in the middle of it all has to matter. As our seconds and minutes and days slip by, something has to be important. And who are we to giggle and point and sneer at what others have found to care about? Who is to say what is beneath us, what is not worthy of our hearts?

Everybody needs something to hold when it gets dark, so who am I, who are any of us, to point at anything and call it facile or redundant or stupid or rubbish?

Even when it quite clearly is.

A new stand up show about finding something important in an ocean of twaddle.
Around every single thing that we know, there are a billion things that we don’t. This show may well be about that. But it is, as I write this, still only April. So who knows?

title: 66a Church Road: A Lament, Made of Memories and Kept in Suitcases

synopsis.text: A poetic lament to a lost love, telling the funny, sad, and nostalgic story of a forsaken flat, a broken heart, and the ache for home.

blurb: A poetic lament to a lost love and a forsaken flat.
development: Showing 24 November – 13 December[23]

blurbs.text:
Last September I had to move out of my flat after living there for almost
six years. I didn’t want to. I lived alone. I am single. 66a Church Road
was the longest relationship of my life.
This is a break up show. For my flat.
Astonishing images emerge from weather-beaten suitcases. Telling a funny, sad and nostalgic story of a forsaken flat. A broken heart. And the ache for home.

title: It's Always Right Now Until It's Later

synopsis.text: A wonderfully written monologue about two ordinary people, Caroline Carpenter and William Rivington, exploring life's moments from birth to death.
Praised for its 'linguistic and visual radiance' and 'jaw-dropping' use of language.

blurb: A radiant monologue on the lives of two ordinary people.

blurbs.text: I wanted to put something amazing where you wouldn't expect to find it. To take the first moments of the morning and fill them with something silly and sad and wonderful. Something audacious in its scope and scale. Something to make you laugh and cry and wonder before the world even knows you're awake. Something to stuff your hearts full and send you out, into the day and into the world, wet eyed and open mouthed. This is a show about every single one of us, the past in our pockets, the future in our hearts and us, ourselves; very much stuck, trapped forever, in the tiny eternal moment between the two. and, it'll all be over in time for lunch.

A new show about everything and nothing. This is a show about every single one of us, the past in our pockets, the future in our hearts and us, ourselves, very much stuck, trapped forever, in the tiny eternal moment between the two. And, it’ll all be over in time for lunch. ‘He represents and inhabits that place where no-one is cool, sussed, glamorous and sorted, and everyone is struggling, somewhere between absurdity and pathos, to put together a life less barren, and more fulfilled’ (Joyce Macmillan).

title: Where Once Was Wonder

synopsis.text: Comprises three major stories about 'The Impossible'. A stand-up show that incorporates storytelling elements.

development:
Performed in 2012: re-staged

Work-in-progress tour around UK in March 2012, then toured Australia and returned for Edinburgh Fringe in August. Comprises three stories about 'the impossible'.

blurbs.text: A new stand-up comedy show about cutting the head off a pig, and something a Spanish footballer once said. Please note: other topics also discussed.

A new stand-up comedy show about impossibility, change, not joining in, love, haircuts, loneliness, courage, defiance, knowledge, despondency, cutting the head off a pig, meaning, tattoos, obscenity, opinions, truth and something a Spanish footballer once said.* Thirty-four years old, predictably fond of snacks, surprisingly capable at sport and increasingly uncertain about absolutely everything, Daniel Kitson - comedian, legend and intermittent tool - tries to work out what he actually thinks about things whilst demanding people pay money to watch. *Other topics may be addressed in addition to or in place of the topics listed here.

title: After the Beginning. Before the End
synopsis: It arrived. The Idea. Just like that. Like a child, late home from school, oblivious to theworry and the panic and the phone calls. It just walked in and sat down like it wasnt even a big deal…Two hours ago I didnt have the idea. Now I do. And its going to be good.
blurb: A reflection on reality, memory, and the self.
development:
Performed in 2013: re-staged

UK tour and a few European dates in 2013.

blurbs.text:
This is a very specific point in time.

I’ve been waiting to have the idea for this show for weeks, for months. A space held open in my head waiting for the idea. For months. I’ve done previews and I’ve booked the tour and I’ve stared at the internet and I’ve made chicken and I’ve tried not to worry. But the idea has not come and I have worried. I’ve worried and doubted and waited more and more and more. But then today, having dropped my dad off at the train station and met my friends for some coffee,

Whilst driving home to write this (very overdue) brochure copy - dreading the thought of heaving half lies and optimistic promises into something vaguely intriguing but not developmentally restrictive - half way home, it happened. Somewhere between East London and South London - It arrived. The Idea. Just like that. Like a child, late home from school, oblivious to the worry and the panic and the phone calls. It just walked in and sat down like it wasn’t even a big deal.

So now I’m typing this in my bedroom because the boy who lives next door is playing the James Bond theme on what I assume to be a trumpet.

And you have to trust me.

Two hours ago I didn’t have the idea. Now I do. And it’s going to be good.

It arrived. The Idea. Just like that. Like a child, late home from school, oblivious to the worry and the panic and the phone calls. It just walked in and sat down like it wasn't even a big deal…

Two hours ago I didn't have the idea. Now I do. And it's going to be good.

title: Tree
development:
early-morning Work in Progress at Latitude 2013, where Kitson had decided on the set, the number of characters and about four pages of decent dialogue.

We got a taster of the first page or so of dialogue, involving a man at the bottom of a tree talking to a character further up in the branches (out of sight) protesting against, and protecting it from, a neighbourhood campaign of felling. He would probably play the bloke in the tree, to spare him getting into costume, Kitson advised us, as he peered at the script on his laptop.

In July the blurb on the Royal Exchange website advised “There is currently no running time, no marketing copy, no programme image,”

blurbs.text:
A show for two people written and directed by Daniel Kitson.

It’s early evening. it’s mid autumn. It’s starting to get dark. And on a quiet residential street somewhere in England, a man with a picnic basket, arrives at a tree.

A new show about dissent, commitment, two people and a tree.

title: Analog.Ue

synopsis.text: An experimental show where the entire 90-minute performance was pre-recorded on tape. Kitson never speaks live during the show.
Divided critics: 'My idea of hell' says The Times, 'Five Stars' says The Telegraph.

blurb: Experimental show performed entirely via pre-recorded tape.
development: Performed in 2014: re-staged

blurbs.text:
A mess of cables. A mound of electronic junk. A single cassette player. And then —

Lights. Power Sockets. Audio Tape. Televisions. Vinyl. Neon Words.
VCR Machines. Speaker cables. Amplifiers. At least one Ladder.
Video Tape. Speakers. Super 8. Microphones. Projectors. And running.

Lots and lots of running.

Daniel Kitson returns to the National Theatre with a new show about a prerecorded story.

blurb: A Variety of Things in a Room
development: With Gavin Osborn

blurbs.text: A Variety of Things in a Room

title: Fuckstorm 3001
development:
One part of the show involved a scripted performance with three audience members, acting as an early prototype for the concept Kitson eventually realised in Polyphony, and plays with themes used later again in Collaborator.

The scene kicks off with a botched attempt at technical choreography as Daniel tries to get three audience members to play tape recorders in sync. Instead of a smooth performance, he’s met with a wall of resistance ranging from technical incompetence and general distraction to a full-blown strike. While one participant is just happy to be there for the naughty late-night vibe and another gets bored enough to start reading the programme mid-show, the most vocal dissenter, A, decides to take Daniel to task for being demeaning.

As the argument deepens, it turns into a sharp, self-aware deconstruction of Kitson’s brand and the subculture of the Edinburgh Fringe. Character A systematically shreds Kitson's image, pointing out that the midnight starts, tiny venues, and "pretentiously low" ticket prices aren't just artistic quirks but logistical hurdles that effectively weed out anyone with a normal job or a family. The debate touches on the "wilful incompetence" of his marketing and the hidden costs of attending a show that demands so much of its audience. By the end, Kitson is forced to drop the act and apologise.

title: A Show for Christmas

blurbs.text:
It's 4.52pm on Wednesday the twenty fourth of December when Pollyanna Plunket, briefly distracted by opening a bag of toffees, glances away from the road for no more than a second only to find, upon looking back that an old man from nowhere made his way to the middle of the street. She hits the horn, the man turns, she stamps hard on the brakes and whilst the vehicle, a mid sized motorhome, does slow down, it does not stop. Skidding on, over glistening black tarmac, careering into the old man making no visible attempt to move – rooted to the spot, bent over under the weight of a battered red bag – the inevitable impact flips him up over the bonnet and into the windscreen, his eyes oddly calm as they meet hers for a moment before he slumps down the glass, rolling back off the bonnet and out of sight.

A story about possibility and magic and grief and hope and tradition and toffees.

Which is to say, Christmas. Basically. 

In 2014 Daniel Kitson was asked by Shelley Hastings at the Battersea Arts Centre if he wanted to write a Christmas show and, much to his surprise, he did.  This is that show. It was performed that Christmas for five nights, in the Grand Hall at BAC and for fourteen nights the following Christmas at the Connelly Theatre in New York.

This year, throughout December he's taking it on tour to a handful of venues around the UK, including here.

Obviously.

Last year, I wrote and performed a Christmas Show called – quite brilliantly – A Show For Christmas. I didn’t know what it was going to be when I agreed to do it but I ended up really liking how it turned out and having only booked a five night run at the Battersea Arts Centre in the now defunct Grand Hall , I was always keen to do it again this year and... 

So, as you may have guessed by now, I am bringing it to New York this Christmas and specifically to the Connelly Theatre in the East Village.
 
The show itself is a simple storytelling affair that is, if you’ll forgive me for saying so, pretty enchanting and deals, amongst other things, with the traditional seasonal concerns of loneliness, a brief scuffle in a newsagents, mobile phones, magic, the past and a long journey in a mid sized motorhome*.
 
I mean. It's long been my vibe to charmingly undersell things, as you doubtless know, but I reread it last week to see if I needed to change anything and I actually REALLY enjoyed it.
 
I think it's really good.
 
So. There you go.

-----Daniel Kitson

reviews.snippet: ...it’s like a jigsaw, and it’s only at the end you can appreciate just how brilliantly designed that jigsaw is.
Daniel Kitson’s characteristically simple show offered up one glittering bauble after another of dialogue, character observation and turn of phrase
In anybody else’s hands this could easily turn into a Planes, Trains and Automobiles epic, but, of course, Kitson has a lot more finesse than that.

development:
in late spring of 2014, Shelley from Battersea Arts Centre asked if I wanted to make a Christmas Show and I found myself thinking: - Yes, oddly, I really do.

So i did. 

I wrote a story about possibility and magic and grief and hope and tradition and toffees. Which is to say, Christmas. Basically.  I performed the show in the Grand Hall of Battersea Arts Centre for five nights in December of 2014. Then at the Connelly Theater in the East Village of New York City for two weeks in December of 2015. Then on a UK tour in December of 2017 - it’s the only old show of mine, that i like returning to and, to be honest I sometimes daydream about turning it into a film or something like that. 

Anyway

It also features Isy Suttie being absolutely brilliant and i couldn’t really have made this show or continued to like it as much as i do without her living round the corner and being brilliant. [1]

title: Polyphony
blurb: A meta-theatrical experiment with audience iPods.
development: Performed in 2015

blurbs.text: Ive written a play with a lot of parts. Each of them pre-recorded in isolation and will, when played back in precise unison, form a glorious theatrical polyphony. I just need enough people to press play. Thats all.

I have written a play – a real humdinger. It has a pretty epic scope, a relatively staggering vision and somewhere in the region of 20 characters. Each of which has been performed and recorded in isolation, producing a litany of individual voices – each of them perfect, captured as a single track that will, when played back in precise unison, form a glorious theatrical polyphony. The play is perfect. The recordings are perfect. I just need enough people to hit play. That’s all.

"I have written a Play.*

It has a pretty epic scope, a relatively grand vision and somewhere in the region of twenty characters. It is, in short, a real humdinger.

Unfortunately though, it is a script so ambitious, so demanding, so exquisitely detailed that handing an actor this text and putting them in front of an audience is much like handing  a three year old child a pint of coffee and putting them in front of a zipwire.

It wont end well.

So I, ever vigilant, have pre-recorded each actor, in isolation, onto a separate tape, their fallibility erased with editing and their odious need for attention mitigated by the removal of the audience. Leaving a litany of individual voices – each one perfect and captured on a tape that when played back in precise unison will form a glorious theatrical polyphony.

The play is perfect.

The tapes are perfect.

I just need enough people to press play. That’s all."

*I directed it as well and I’ve got the main part too.

title: Something Other Than Everything
development:
Performed in 2017

Kitson's first stand-up in over four years. Performed at The Roundhouse (July 2017) and The Royal Exchange, Manchester. Largely well-reviewed, though received criticism from a Guardian journalist regarding in-context language.

blurbs.text: Marooned in the middle of a circle of microphones, speakers, lights and people he will attempt to not simply dismantle and rebuild his own ideas about compassion, isolation, solidarity and progress but in doing so momentarily alter the function of language and invent a new form of stand-up comedy.

blurbs.text:
A brand-new story told entirely through the peripheries and pivot points of an as-yet undetermined number of debates, wrangles, quarrels, arguments, discussions, tiffs, altercations, contretemps, and squabbles.
Daniel Kitson, a well-regarded but bald-headed forty-year-old writer and performer from a small village in the north of England, visits Washington DC for the very first time with the world premiere of a brand new show written in this particular time, for this particular place, and likely to be both funny and thoughtful, absurd and serious, rich with humanity and riddled with frustration.

title: Good For Glue

dates.wipNote: titled 'Not really work in progress. More just something to do.'

blurbs.text: Because he's an idiot, in thrall to his own imagined past, Daniel Kitson (41) has decided to perform an unfinished show that starts at midnight in a room that gets debilitatingly hot and doesn't have enough chairs. In terms of tone, the closest comparison is probably that bit in Oldboy with the corridor full of baddies and the hammer.

development: Scripts for the earliest performances included experimental elements such as dialogue with an off-stage, pre-recorded voice. These concepts ultimately didn't work out as planned, and Kitson cut them from the show after just two runs. These scripts are available on An Accumulation of Failure.[1]

title: Outside. Again.

development:
Daniel reveals he's recently begun a pottery course and showcases a couple of examples of his work on stage.

Gavin plays a couple of songs from his 2022 album Scrolling Home alongside others, including a spoken word track to his past self, and John's Not Got a TV[1], which have not yet seen a commercial release.

blurbs.text:
*There was a time when we came here every year, to sing songs amongst the trees, and tell tales beneath the stars to fill the sky with a bonfire built from laughter and love and joy and wonder.
 
But that was years ago now.
 
Back when life was a gathering glory of boundless potential. Back when simplistic sadness felt profound and broken hearts were plot points and death was just a narrative device. Back when a night’s sleep could stretch out beyond noon and eating crispy shredded beef after midnight had minimal digestive repercussions the following day. Back before births and deaths and distance and doubt. Before beliefs buckled and hope came to feel facile and pyjamas started to almost always smell at least a little bit of piss.
 
And yet.
 
Here we are again.
 
In the middle of the night in the middle of the summer.
 
Under the sky.
 
In a park.
 
Rummaging in the rubble for something to burn, a forgotten fragment of anything flammable, searching for something to set alight and hold aloft and to fling it, together, once again, out and up into the deepening dark.*
 
Show will include: Sad songs, funny stories, indefensible declarations, crosstalk, technical difficulties, sad stories, moths, funny songs, quite possibly a chant and at least two, standard lamps.

footnotes: A song about Gav's intimidation on learning the poet and author John Osborne does not own a television set.

title: Everything Smells Of Orange
development: Announced mid-July, Kitson took the slot vacated by Paul Sinha, who cancelled his Fringe run after being diagnosed with Parkinson's disease.

blurbs.text: Daniel Kitson, having not made anything unequivocally good for years and years and years, now displays a charmless lack of respect for a tolerant if dwindling audience, a supportive venue and, actually, the greatest arts festival in the world, by filling a slot at the last minute, doing very little work, making no discernible progress and charging everyone five pounds to watch.

title: Shenanigan

characters.name: Penelope Ruttell
characters.description: Woman who is gaslit by her ex-boyfriend

characters.name: Robert Duncan
characters.description: Man who gaslights his ex-girlfriend

characters.name: Claire
characters.description: Friend of Bob and Poppy's who had dinner with them

characters.name: Gilllian
characters.description: Actor hired by Bob to pretend she works at Snappy Snaps

characters.name: Martin
characters.description: Actor hired by Bob to pretend he works as a security guard

characters.name: Paul
characters.description: Man who helps Bob print pictures

characters.name: William
characters.description: Friend of Bob and Poppy's who had dinner with them

blurbs.text:
On most Tuesdays, I go to a café before football for lunch with my friends Tim and Isy. Last week, the café had a really appealing special on the board which involved roasted cauliflower, pickled cabbage and babaganoush. The lady behind the counter referred to it as “The Shenanigan”.

Which I very much enjoyed.

This show will have absolutely nothing to do with that meal, that lady, that café, those friends, playing football or Tuesdays but I have to call it something otherwise they can’t put it in the system.

Something new, vaguely experimental, unfinished and frankly, quite unlikely to ever be finished, by Daniel Kitson.

I did a work in progress storytelling show in the Paines Plough Roundabout at Summerhall called Shenanigan. I liked the story a lot and wanted to do more work on it and had, actually, in fact, if you must know, been in touch with a couple of theatres about potentially turning it into a big old proper show over the course of last year.

But then things happened and here we are.

The plan now is to do these two weeks worth of shows, to both work on the story itself and experiment a bit with the telling of it – to see whether it can function entirely as a piece of audio stuff. So, it’s fundamentally the same story as two years ago told in a new way which means I’ve called it – quite brilliantly – Shenaniganagain.

development:
Daniel Kitson named the show "Shenanigan" simply because he overheard someone say the word in a cafe and liked the sound of it. Before the pandemic, he ran it as a work-in-progress storytelling piece at the Trades Club in Hebden Bridge and the Paines Plough Roundabout in Edinburgh. He was really happy with how it was shaping up - a solid mystery centered around two people - and was in talks with a few theatres about turning it into a full stage production.

When the pandemic shut things down, those stage plans were immediately scrapped. Instead, Kitson spent a couple of weeks experimenting to see if the piece could work purely as an audio experience. Since it was the exact same core story just delivered in a new format, he gave it a new title: "Shenaniganagain."

During his online audio tests, he realized the piece was ultimately a story about telling stories, and he loved how well it translated to the new medium. His final plan was to finish the writing over the late summer and record it three separate times: once for LPs, once for CDs, and once for cassette tapes. Kitson explicitly stated that the finished version would never be performed live, nor would it be made available as a digital download

title: Keep

synopsis.text: Kitson attempts to read aloud a catalogued list of 20,000 objects in his home. The show uses this premise to explore the weight of objects and memory.

development:
Run at Battersea Arts Centre. Premise: reading aloud a catalogued list of 20,000 objects in his home, with narrative and humour.

The stand-up material from this originated in Good For Glue - Kitson's run of shows at the Edinburgh Fringe in 2018[1]

blurbs.text:
A new show about how much past the present can usefully contain.

About rigour and generosity. About postcards and hair pins and a certificate from Harry Ramsdens in Blackpool. About how long it takes to stop noticing where you are. About the compromise of a full life and the burden of a full heart and how it’s impossible to know where looking back will lead. About the task of being who we are without denying who we’ve been. About the importance of regret and the possibility of hope and the delusional idea of starting again. About all the books I’ve never read and all the jam I’ve ever eaten and the bags of torn tickets and the drawers of empty pens and the inevitable sadness of ever holding on to anything.

About, in short, the stuff in my house and the thoughts in my head.

title: Dot. Dot. Dot.
blurb: A pandemic show of Post-its and Polaroids.
development:
Streamed online to ticket holders

Toured during COVID-19 pandemic to venues without live audiences, streamed to ticket-holders. Performed with Post-it Notes and Polaroid photos covering vignettes from the pandemic.

The performance from the Union Chapel was later made available on *An Accumulation of Failure*.

blurbs.text:
A new show for empty theatres.

*I have not been operating within the guidelines and I have not been guided by the science and I have not come out the other side stronger or wiser or better for the experience. I didn’t eat out to help out and I didn’t become a parent and I have not made use of a reciprocal travel corridor. I haven’t moved out of London and I haven’t volunteered for anything and I haven’t attended a socially distanced outdoor performance of Educating Rita. I didn’t keep going to work and I didn’t work remotely and I did not use this time to work on my physique. I haven’t done laps of my garden and I haven’t put myself in harms way and I have done nothing to support the British hospitality industry. I did not miss out on medical treatment. I did not learn a language. I did not postpone a wedding. I did not book a staycation in Cornwall or the Lakes or somewhere on the Kent coast. I didn’t march and I didn’t go swimming and I didn’t get tested and I did not “read more”. I’ve not been on a boat. I’ve not been shielding, I’ve not been struggling with childcare, I’ve not been playing football on Tuesdays, I’ve not been killed and I’ve not been attending gatherings of up to six households. I haven’t lost my job, I haven’t attended a funeral, I haven’t used a foodbank and I haven’t touched my parents since Christmas.*

Daniel Kitson presents his account of six ridiculous and devastating months, experienced largely from a safe distance, almost immediately misremembered and retold here so inaccurately as to be, very nearly fictional. Conceived and written specifically to be performed in and streamed from empty theatres with audience capacity limited to that of each venue, this will hopefully be a lot more fun than it sounds.

title: Outside.
development:
Return to the stage after 2 years. Toured outdoor venues in the UK. Despite the blurb, Gavin Osborn only joined for the Regents Park shows.

Recorded over the three final tour dates, but this has not yet been released on any platform.

blurbs.text:
Daniel hasn’t been on stage for over two years.

And, to be entirely honest, he’s not really missed it.

It is, however, his actual job and everyone’s gone back to work now. So, he’s picked out a comfy pen, bought a new notebook and booked himself a summers worth of outdoor shows to find out whether he can still do his job and what, if anything, he has to say to large groups of people he doesn’t know.

Please note: What with the two year gap and the lack of practice this entire tour will be, to a greater or lesser extent, work in progress and the shows are likely to be relatively rickety affairs. But on the up side, they will sometimes feature support and/or collaboration from Gavin Osborn (when his schedule allows) and Daniel’s already written the question “Do worms feel fear?” in his new notebook, so we should be okay.

Also, if it gets boring – you can just use the time to look at the sky and feel small.

title: Maybe a Ghost Story

characters.name: Graham
characters.description: Man driving to his childhood home on Halloween

characters.name: Roger
characters.description: Man who lives near Graham's childhood home

development: Performed on 26 and 27 October 2022 at Shakespeare's Globe, London

blurbs.text:
*I don’t believe in Ghosts and I’m not really comfortable with (or good at) frightening people. 

One Halloween, more than forty years ago, me, my mum and my brother turned off every light in the house and waited just inside the front door, for my dad to get home from work so we could frighten him. We’d made some frightening lanterns from turnips and learned a pretty frightening song about witches.

But when my Dad walked in he wasn’t frightened. 

He was, if anything, visibly relieved.  

Now, this was a long time ago so I don’t remember all the details but apparently, my Dad, having pulled round the corner to see the house in total darkness was confused and a little concerned as to the whereabouts and, by extension, the wellbeing of his family. So, walking in to find two small boys brandishing candle lit vegetables and singing a song about how they were the ugliest witches he’d ever seen was in an odd way, quite reassuring.

Later, he said he’d been worried by all the lights being off and I felt bad about causing that worry.

I still do.*

A singularly seasonal story by Daniel Kitson that is definitely funny, potentially frightening and maybe, just maybe, a ghost story.

*I don’t believe in Ghosts and I’ve never been comfortable with (or really, any good at) frightening people.

One Halloween, forty years ago, me, my mum and my brother turned off every light in the house and waited just inside the front door, for my dad to get home from work so we could frighten him. We’d made frightening lanterns from turnips and we’d learned a frightening song about witches.

But when my Dad walked in he wasn’t frightened.

He was, if anything, visibly relieved.

Apparently, having found the house in complete darkness he’d immediately been concerned as to the whereabouts and wellbeing of his family. Which meant that opening the door to find his sons waggling candle lit turnips and singing about being witches was, actually, quite close to comforting.

He said later, that he’d been worried by all the lights being off and I felt bad about that.

I still do.*

A new show by Daniel Kitson written specifically for these two nights in this one place that will be funny, might be frightening and may, in spite of everything, be a ghost story.

title: I Shall Have a Good Think When Everybody’s Gone Home
blurb: I Shall Have a Good Think When Everybody’s Gone Home

blurbs.text:
This isn’t *really* a work in progress as much as it is just a loose dollop of stand up in search of an idea. 

It shouldn’t be *awful* though and has been priced accordingly.

I know that’s not massively appealing without an existing knowledge of my powerful back catalogue and that maybe you’d rather catch a cool flick, or track down some decent noodles or seek out a magical first kiss by the river followed by a lifetime of happiness.

Honestly?

I wouldn’t blame you, that sounds lovely.

Its up to you. Your times your own.

This is the bit before you get to an actual Work in Progress but it shouldn't be awful and has been priced accordingly. Also, crucially, it'll be done nice and early, so you'll have plenty of time to salvage your day with a better show by someone else or some decent noodles or a first kiss followed by a life time of happiness. It's up to you. Your time's your own. ⁠

development: Preceded by a series of shows at The Bill Murray in Islington and The Trades Club in Hebden Bridge from January to March 2023 titled *The Bit Before You Get To A Work In Progress (Priced Accordingly)*.

title: Collaborator

characters.name: Keith
characters.aka: Audience member who argues with Daniel Kitson

blurbs.text:
I dislike audience participation.

I think it’s creatively bankrupt, ethically questionable and if I see it mentioned in the blurb for a show, I will not attend due to moral objections and/or personal discomfort.

Anyway.

I’ve written something about that and it’s got just over 200 parts.

So…

Collaborator was conceived specifically to be performed in the round and is re-written a bit for each particular venue.

I dislike audience participation.

I think it’s creatively bankrupt, ethically questionable and if I see it mentioned in the blurb for a show, I will not attend due to moral objections and/or personal discomfort.

Anyway.

I’ve written something about that and it’s got just over 200 parts.

So…

Collaborator was conceived specifically to be performed in the round and is re-written a bit for each particular venue.

development: The show music was released on *An Accumulation of Failure*. The album, titled *Clumsy Child*, is likely a reference to a misdiagnosis Daniel received as a child of the condition now more commonly known as dyspraxia.

title: Pith

characters.name: Claire
characters.description: Woman who meets Mr. Pickles on a bus

characters.name: Meredith Winters
characters.description: Robert Pickles' late wife, who met him on a bench

development:
19 Jan 2024, DK Newsletter 
“I am talking to the Exchange about hopefully doing something else there at some point in the next 18 months or so because It’s the absolute best place.”

27 Jul 2024, BBC London Radio
Kitson is apparently about to start writing something that he'll premiere in Manchester in October.

11 Sept 2024, DK Newsletter
"... i’m going to be sending another email in the next few days announcing the first run of a new show in Manchester for later this Autumn”

11 Sept 2024, Royal Exchange website, 
Blurb, poster and dates announced. Tickets “Available from Fri 13 Sep”

17,18,19 Previews
21, 22, 23, 24, 25 (evenings), 26 (evening & matinee)

14th Sept 2024
26th October matinee unlisted
Royal Exchange announces that the whole run of 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' (Mon 9 Sep – Sat 12 Oct 2024) has now been cancelled citing “a number of issues with the production”

16 Sept 2024
Revealed that after a preview on Sept 6, theatre bosses called for section of MND which included a song referencing trans rights and the phrase “free Palestine”. Director Stef O’Driscoll disagreed.
Pith disappears entirely from the Royal Exchange website

19 Sept 2024
Two WIP at Bill Murray announced for 21/22 September under the title Standing Start.
“Daniel has to have a new show ready in less than four weeks. In spite of sitting at his desk a bit, he hasn't really got started yet so the idea of these shows is that they will hopefully be so bad he'll be terrified into some clarity of thought in terms of the writing process, moving forward. Yum yum.”

25 Sept 2024
PITH goes on sale again. Including the matinee on the 26th October

blurbs.text:
There *might* be music.

There might be music and the phrase “You will one day”, and a man in a coffee shop asking if they charge for Babycinos. There might be someone’s hand in mine when I wake up and the sound of somewhere else and a notebook full of crossed out names. There might be more trains than usual needing to be repaired at the same time. There might be cake and natural causes and a ladybird walking across the screen of a smartphone. There might be other ideas and a dog with a haircut and my reflection inside a fitting room. There might be gaps in the stories and stories in the gaps and work to be done. There might be a new thought and an old friend and someone else’s coat too close to my leg. There might be clues and there might be carpet and there might be a busker in Cardiff singing hallelujah.

And,

It’s possible,

There *might* be jokes.

Daniel Kitson is returning to the Manchester Royal Exchange for the first time since 2017 with a brand new show about having ideas, telling stories and starting again, again.

title: Fuckstorm 3000
development:
Andy Zalzman spoke to Vulture on how the show came about[1]

We first did it in 2006, the show called “Honourable Men of Art”, which was gonna be him with John and me and Alun, and a couple others – Demetri Martin did it then, and David O’Doherty. And then John got the Daily Show job, so he would be on a screen via the Internet a couple of times, when he was able to do it. I guess the idea was just to have a show where it was unprepared and spontaneous and just see what happened.

It’s just three guys messing around, really. Kitson is certainly the greatest comedian of my generation here, and so me and Alun just sort of chip in every now and again, but he’s a phenomenon, so it’s fun just to ride in his wake. And the nature of any show like that, it’s a bit up and down but it’s been good fun to do and people have mostly seemed to have enjoyed it. Although I did get a comment on Twitter saying that was the worst comedy show I have ever seen. But people see things in very different ways, that’s one of the joys of the art form.

blurbs.text:
Founder members of seminal comedy bomb The Honourable Men of Art and alumni of one off mega hit, Zock! now bring you Fuckstorm 3000. Andy (politics and hair) Daniel (banter and blue) Alun (not a postman) and Gavin (ditties, giggling and eye for a pass) are about to hit you with a force ten fuck storm. Fuckstorm 3000 - It does exactly what it says on the tin*

* -Only true if the tin has the words “four men dicking about for a bit, with an interval” written on it.

title: Thrum

blurbs.text:
There’s the sound of somewhere else and the permanence of a plant pot and the ubiquity of a phrase and the heartbreaking brevity of a very long time. There’s figs and worms and an old dog rejecting a blanket. There’s a woman, on a train, wearing a badge. There’s the first beep of a smoke alarm and there’s a daffodil in December and there’s the condition of fruit. There’s a hand in bed and there’s stuff on the stairs and there’s a mark on my jumper and there’s a massive tree in storage on a farm in Kent. There’s nothing to say and there’s the thought of rotting and there’s the name of a feeling and there’s the smell of tobacco. There’s the twelfth floor of a hotel and the far end of a platform at Tamworth train station. There’s a duty to act and there’s a resistance to growth and there’s a new place up the road and there’s a completely redundant volume control. There’s a toilet in Hong Kong and a coffee shop in Melbourne and the passage of light through a building and deliberately making things much more difficult than they actually need to be.

*And.*

There’s music, more or less throughout, at least two funny voices and upwards of three jokes.

Daniel Kitson is working on a new show.

He’s hoping, as per usual, to make something funny, maybe a bit sad, intermittently thought provoking, slightly audacious and, if at all possible, largely unfathomable.

He started properly thinking about it in the middle of January and it needs to be properly finished by August. So. That should give you a sense of how far through the process these particular shows are likely to be in terms of the lump of stone to finished sculpture ratio.

On the upside, they’ve been priced accordingly and will last no more than 90 minutes.

I’m going to start the year by waddling over the hill to Stanley Halls and doing a bunch of very (very) early stage work in progress towards something that feels like it might, potentially, become some sort of stand up comedy show, at some point, possibly.

It’ll just be me trying out things and blathering on, working out what I think, how much of what I think I want to say and how I want to say whatever amount of what I think, I decide I want to say. There’s also some lovely tiled walls to look at if you get bored.
It’ll cost five pounds and last somewhere between 60 and 90 minutes.

dates.wipNote: Some sort of work in progress thing
Some sort of work in progress thing
Some sort of work in progress thing
Stand-up at the Subway comedy festival

development:
Title, blurb and poster were announced on Kitson's site on the 13th May.

The blurb is very, *very* similar to Pith's.

Both blurbs rely on anaphora, switching between "There might be" and "There’s". They share imagery of holding hands in bed, alongside an exact phrase match with "the sound of somewhere else" popping up in both. Both weave in observations about trains, and both end with a tentative promise of jokes.

A short list of just five Edinburgh Fringe dates was announced 15th May. They would go on sale 18th May to be followed with "a UK tour with something in the region of 35 dates throughout the autumn."

title: Belle & Sebastian Tour Support
synopsis: Not much about this story is known. In a review of the Bristol show, it was described as an "enchantingly detailed tale of a schoolboy's love ... ending in a to-be-continued-style resolution."
development: Following their performance at Latitude Festival in 2010, Kitson announced in mid-November that he and Gavin Osborn had been tapped to support Belle and Sebastian on their UK tour. To kick off the nights, the pair wrote a special 35-minute opening set combining their signature mix of story and music.[1]
